title: Economics joins the culture war
full_text:
BUDGET 2026 As the culture wars reignited over summer following the Bondi massacre and runaway support for One Nation, Jim Chalmers was quietly given the all-clear to start working up a budget that would become the most controversial in decades.
“We were thinking about tax reform over the summer,” the Treasurer said. “There wasn’t a moment where we flicked the switch … but the decision was taken relatively recently to change our policy.” On Tuesday and Thursday nights this week, Chalmers’ budget and Angus Taylor’s budget reply respectively triggered their own culture war over economics and the household budget through tax reform.
The fundamental division in this new culture war over economics is based on two schools of thought.
Chalmers, influenced by left-leaning economics professor Joseph Stiglitz, whom he interviewed in February, is channelling the Marx and Engels view of the world: that economic decisions can shape cultural ones. He announced significant changes, including the broken promises of scrapping the 50 per cent capital gains tax discount for all asset classes in favour of inflation indexation, the removal of negative gearing, the introduction of a 30 per cent minimum tax on CGT and distributions from trusts, and a new Working Australians Tax Offset that gives wage and salary earners only a $250 sweetener.
For Chalmers, this is the core principle used to address the “welcome debate” that seeks to “rebalance this issue we’ve got between very generous treatment of assets and less generous treatment of labour income of workers”.
The Opposition Leader, who is influenced more by right-leaning economics professor John Cochrane, on Thursday channelled the Friedrich Hayek view of the world that he quoted at the National Press Club early this year: that the cultural side of a society is what shapes economic decisions.
Taylor, who backs the risk-taking, wealth-creating culture of Australians with lower tax and smaller government, announced a reform package that would repeal Chalmers’ capital gains tax changes and negative gearing, and instead fully scrap bracket creep – where inflation pushes people into higher average tax rates over time.
Taylor committed to indexing to inflation the bottom two tax thresholds, protecting 85 per cent of income earners, from 2028-29, then indexing the top two thresholds from 2031-32, leaving all taxpayers protected from inflation.
Chalmers’ tax plan would raise $100bn in new revenue over the medium term, leaving the government with a net $77bn in tax revenue. Chalmers also takes the unwanted title of becoming Australia’s highest income-taxing Treasurer since records were kept.
Budget paper figures show that total income tax as a share of GDP will hit 18.6 per cent in 2025-26 and 2026-27, staying at similarly high levels for the next three years. While the $100bn in new tax over the medium term is small in the overall $7 trillion in tax the government will collect over the next 10 years, these changes will not only have broader economic impacts, they also will enliven and test the way people think and act at barbecues and the offices of family accountants and financial advisers.
H&R Block Australia director of tax communications Mark Chapman says this is a big moment for Australia. “These changes will change people. Just look at what happens this weekend at property auctions,” he says. “The tax changes in the budget will change the way people invest and run their businesses. People will become more conservative by investing in income as opposed to capital growth.” Mark Bouris, the Australian businessman best known as the founder and chairman of Wizard Home Loans and now chairman of Yellow Brick Road, which has written more than $320bn in loans, says the changes are an “existential moment for Australia”.
“This is a very important cultural moment because all the people I deal with, all the small businesses, are not the wealthiest people in the country, they are the people who are trying to build wealth,” he says.
“This is an existential moment in Australian history, and if you are a small business (the Treasurer’s changes will be diabolic).” Bouris says one of the key concerns is that people under the age of 40 who are coming new to negative gearing will now be prevented from using the wealth creation tools from which the older generation has benefited.
Most people under 40 who are taking the risk of trying to build wealth, and invest in property, negatively gear that property by offsetting their rental losses on an investment property against their income.
Under the Chalmers plan, those new investors will be precluded from doing so unless they buy new builds. Treasury’s own modelling in the budget shows that such tax changes, including the removal of the 50 per cent CGT discount, will cut 35,000 houses from the supply across the next few years.
But the government and some economists say more money will flow to new supply. Corinna Economic Advisory’s Saul Eslake says taking out the investors who negatively gear and benefit from the CGT discount will mean those who have pushed up prices and pushed others out of the housing market will now create space for first-time buyers to come into the market.
But former Reserve Bank economist Peter Tulip, now at the Centre for Independent Studies, says the credibility of these economists is on the line.
“The Greens, @TheAusInstitute, @AlanKohler, @SaulEslake and many others have argued that tax concessions have had large effects on housing prices,” he posted on X. “They should now be forecasting large reductions in house prices. This is a test of their credibility.” Red and Co’s David Laverty, one of the bigger private property developers in Queensland, says if you are aspirational, you are now not getting as much of a benefit as those who have been aspirational.
“The people they hate the most who have built wealth, they’ve gone and grandfathered these policies for them, so the young guys can’t replicate what the older generation did when they were young,” he says.
Laverty says the lack of clarity on all the rules, especially changes to trusts, means the housing market will slow and rents will rise.
“When people don’t know how to structure a deal, they don’t do a deal,” he says. Then there is Chalmers’ removal of the 50 per cent capital gains tax discount on all assets. Here, there are much wider consequences for the broader economy than just people’s personal wealth creation.
David Paradice, one of the highest-profile fund managers in Australia, says investors won’t invest in high-growth businesses because of the tax and instead will go for high-yielding businesses that do not reinvest capital, which is essential for boosting productivity in Australia.
“It is a political risk. It is going to change the culture,” he says. “I spent years overseas in places like South America where politics was a big factor in investment decisions. Australia, up until recently, you could make investment decisions without too much consideration about what government was doing. That’s not the case (any) more.
“If you go for growth you are taxed double, so people will invest in yield instead, but the higher-yielding stocks don’t put as much back into the business ... it prevents companies from growing and raising capital.” Chalmers has given some sweeteners to businesses, such as two years of loss carryback and making permanent the instant asset write-off of up to $20,000 a year. This initiative cost the government roughly $300m a year. Taylor matched him on the instant asset write-off, raising it to $50,000 a year permanently.
The third big plank in Chalmers’ announcement is his approach to giving back some of the bracket creep in this budget.
The Treasurer announced the WATO, which gives every wage and salary earner who pays tax about half of what they lose in bracket creep a year.
Chalmers’ plan starts from 2028 – the next election year.
Taylor’s plan for indexation also starts from 2028.
That’s just as well given that government spending, at close to record 40-year highs as a percentage of GDP, outside the pandemic, is pumping up inflation and part of the reason behind the RBA decision to lift interest rates three times this year. Economists such as AMP’s Shane Oliver, HSBC’s Paul Bloxham, Westpac’s Pat Bustamante and UBS’s George Tharenou all say government spending is too high and pushes inflation higher, forcing the RBA to raise rates further.
Any big tax cut would only exacerbate the risk of higher rates, which the older generation experienced in the 1980s.
Wilson Asset Management’s Geoff Wilson, the architect behind the campaign against Bill Shorten’s failed tax changes at the 2019 election, says all the changes are a major cultural test for Australia and one he will be gathering an army to fight. “This year, the debate over aspiration, wealth creation and opportunity will not only shape markets and budgets, it will shape the national mood and the country our children inherit,” he says. “Cost-of-living pressures, falling living standards and the growing belief that younger Australians are working harder while getting further behind are turning economics into a cultural battle.”
summary: Treasurer Jim Chalmers has proposed major tax reforms in the 2026 budget, including changes to capital gains tax and negative gearing, sparking a cultural debate on economics. Opposition Leader Angus Taylor plans to repeal these changes, leading to a significant divide in economic policy perspectives.
author: Matthew Cranston
heading: Banking and Economy
key_points: Chalmers' budget aims to raise $100 billion in new tax revenue.
Key reforms include scrapping the 50% capital gains tax discount and removing negative gearing.
Taylor's response includes repealing Chalmers' changes and addressing bracket creep.
Chalmers becomes Australia's highest income-taxing Treasurer in history.
The tax changes may impact housing supply and investment strategies.
ticker_line: Tax reforms ignite cultural debate in Australia
